title: VPC security guardrails Terraform module: SGs, NACLs, private DNS, egress policies
preview:
Create a reusable, audit-ready Terraform module (registry-style) that implements Redwood private/VPC security guardrails: managed security group templates, stateless/ stateful NACL patterns, private hosted zone / split-horizon DNS integration, and restrictive egress policies with configurable passthroughs. The module must be compatible with our Private deployment automation (VPC creation, subnets, and routing) and provide clear variables for environment-specific overrides, explicit deny-by-default behavior, and out-of-band exceptions for partner integrations. Deliverables: 1) Terraform module source with examples, 2) upgrade guide for existing stacks, 3) health-check plans and unit/integration tests, 4) documentation for Console and onboarding engineers, 5) automated policy checks (Sentinel/OPA) and CI linting.

Goals and constraints:
- Provide secure defaults (least privilege egress, locked-down ingress) while allowing controlled exceptions.
- Support multiple cloud accounts/regions and cross-account VPC peering use cases.
- Backwards-compatible variable names where feasible; offer migration helpers when breaking changes are necessary.
- Expose clearly documented outputs (security_group_ids, acl_ids, private_dns_zone_id, egress_policy_version) to be consumed by other modules (e.g., runtime, bastion, private-control-plane).
- Must integrate with Redwood KMS/HSM and audit logging pipeline for any resource that stores identifiers or metadata.

Acceptance criteria:
- Module passes linting (tflint, checkov), unit tests via Terraform validate/plan-for-target, and integration tests in a staging account.
- Example compositions: "minimal-provision" (default-deny egress), "managed-bastion" (SSH jump with limited ports), "model-serving" (allow egress to S3 & package registries).
- Upgrade guide documents safe migration steps + automated migration tool (terraform state mv examples) for at least two breaking-change scenarios.
- Health checks: automated periodic validation job that compares running SG/NACL state to module-specified intended state and emits diffs to Console.
- Security review sign-off recorded and checklist items closed.

Notes on design tradeoffs:
- Centralized module vs. small focused modules: prefer single module with sub-modules to reduce duplication while keeping per-concern ownership (sg_module, nacl_module, dns_module, egress_module).
- Use tag/metadata conventions to allow Console to surface which stacks are managed by the module and to enable targeted rollbacks.
- Egress policies should be versioned; runtime can opt into an approved version. Versioning reduces blast radius during policy updates.

Interfaces and variables (high level):
- variable "env" (string), variable "allowed_egress_cidrs" (list), variable "egress_whitelist_services" (list of service DNS names or endpoints), variable "private_dns_zone_name" (string), variable "enable_vpc_peering" (bool), variable "exceptions" (list) with a strict schema.
- outputs: security_group_ids, nacl_ids, private_dns_zone_id, egress_policy_version, applied_rules_summary_url.

Upgrade and rollout plan:
1) Implement module in a feature branch and publish to our internal registry under a beta version.
2) Run integration tests in a single non-production AWS account.
3) Onboard two internal teams (Private control plane and Dedicated infra) to test.
4) Gradually promote to production(default) via feature flags and release notes; provide rollback playbook.

Health checks and monitoring:
- Add Terraform-driven resource tags consumed by a periodic auditor lambda that compares AWS SG/NACL entries to the module configuration and writes findings to the Console incident/alerts stream.
- Provide a health-check shell script (or GH action) that runs terraform plan against a read-only data-only workspace to detect drift.
- Define alert thresholds and runbooks (e.g., unexpected wide egress => page on-call SRE).

Testing plan:
- Unit: tflint, terraform validate, checkov policies run in CI.
- Integration: apply module in ephemeral staging VPC, run connectivity tests (from bastion to target services) and negative tests for denied egress.
- Regression: run against representative customer stacks to ensure no breaking behavior.

Documentation & onboarding:
- Confluence PRD + examples in GitHub (./examples/minimal-provision, ./examples/model-serving).
- Console docs: how to interpret applied_rules_summary_url and how to request exceptions.

Rollout blockers:
- Pending decisions: default egress allow-list for package mirrors (internal vs public), cross-account peering exception process, and how to surface exceptions in Console.
- Required approvals: Security review, Infra platform lead sign-off, and Legal for cross-account data movement if needed.

title: Audit log schema: include authz decision context + ensure SIEM export compatibility
preview:
Context / problem
Enterprise customers (Dedicated + Private) require evidence-grade auditability of authorization enforcement. Today we log “what happened” (API call / admin action), but we do not consistently log the authorization decision that allowed/denied the action. This is a gap for SOC2 evidence, incident response, and SIEM correlation (“why was access denied/allowed, under which policy version?”).

This ticket covers:
1) Extending audit log schema to include authz decision context.
2) Ensuring the audit-log exporter includes the same context (and maps cleanly to common SIEM fields).
3) Rollout and backward compatibility (don’t break existing consumers).

Goals
- Every security-relevant request that hits an authz check should emit an audit event with authz decision context (allow/deny) + minimal explanation suitable for operators.
- Decision context must be consistent across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.
- Export pipeline must preserve the context and support customer SIEM ingestion.

Non-goals
- Building a full “policy explain” feature for end users (covered by denial reason codes + Console UX work).
- Logging secrets or sensitive token material in audit logs.

References / artifacts
- Confluence: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/authz-decision-logging-schema.json
- GitHub PR (platform): sources/github/redwood/pr-28292-audit-log-authz-decision-fields.json
- GitHub PR (exporter): sources/github/audit-log-exporter/pr-77-include-authz-context-in-siem-export.json
- Related denial reasons work: sources/github/redwood/pr-28241-add-denial-reason-codes-and-debug-header.json

Proposed schema additions (high level)
Add a set of authz fields to audit events emitted by API Gateway + Control Plane services.

Required fields (for events that perform an authorization check)
- authz.decision: ALLOW | DENY
- authz.action: permission string evaluated (e.g., redwood.org.roles.update)
- authz.scope: evaluated scope (org/project/environment/cluster identifiers as a single canonical string)
- authz.policy_version: policy bundle version/hash used for evaluation
- authz.subject: normalized subject identifier (human user, service account, API key principal)
- authz.subject_type: USER | SERVICE_ACCOUNT | API_KEY | SYSTEM

Recommended fields
- authz.denial_reason_code: stable reason code when decision=DENY (e.g., MISSING_PERMISSION, SCOPE_MISMATCH, EXPLICIT_DENY, POLICY_NOT_FOUND)
- authz.evaluator: where evaluation occurred (GATEWAY | SERVICE | BOTH) for debugging / evidence
- authz.latency_ms: evaluation latency (float or int) for performance tracking
- authz.request_id: correlation id if not already present in event envelope

PII / data handling notes
- Do NOT log raw tokens, API keys, or group membership contents.
- subject should be stable IDs (user_id, service_account_id) rather than emails, where possible. If email is included in the standard envelope today, keep existing behavior but do not add new PII.
- scope string must not include customer data content; identifiers only.

Event coverage requirements
- Control Plane administrative APIs (org/user/role/binding management; routing policy edits; model pin/rollout changes; audit export initiation; retention/residency config).
- API Gateway routes where we enforce authz (esp. streaming routes + any “sensitive” mutations).
- Break-glass usage events should include authz context + explicit break-glass indicator via existing event type (separate ticket tracks procedure).

Implementation plan
1) Define canonical field names + types in the shared audit event schema package.
   - Align naming with Confluence schema doc; keep field names flat in the emitted JSON (exporter expects top-level keys).
   - Backward compatible: additive fields only.

2) Populate authz fields at the source of decision.
   - Gateway: middleware attaches authz context to request context; audit emitter reads from context.
   - Services: interceptor attaches authz context; prefer to log service-side decision for service-enforced checks.
   - If BOTH enforce, log “evaluator=BOTH” but ensure we do not double-emit separate audit events for a single request.

3) Exporter mapping
   - Ensure exporter includes new fields in payload.
   - Add mapping guidance for common SIEM schemas (Splunk, Elastic, Sentinel):
     - authz.decision -> outcome / event.result
     - authz.action -> event.action / privilege
     - authz.subject + subject_type -> user.id / user.type (or actor.*)
     - authz.policy_version -> rule.version / policy.id
     - denial_reason_code -> error.code / event.reason

4) Telemetry + monitoring
   - Dashboard counters: audit events with authz.decision missing for endpoints labeled “requires_authz”.
   - Deny rate tracking by action + scope (already planned for rollout dashboards; ensure fields are present).

5) Testing
   - Unit tests: schema validation + serialization.
   - Integration tests (with RBAC v2 feature flag on):
     - denied request produces audit event with decision=DENY and denial_reason_code.
     - allowed request produces audit event with decision=ALLOW and policy_version.
   - Exporter snapshot tests to ensure new fields survive transformation.

Acceptance criteria
- [x] Schema doc updated + reviewed by Security (Marcus) and GRC (Olga) for evidence suitability.
- [x] Platform emits authz fields for Control Plane and Gateway authz checks.
- [x] Exporter includes authz context fields and does not drop/rename them unexpectedly.
- [x] No breaking changes for existing audit log consumers (additive fields only; exporter versioned release).
- [x] Sample “allow” and “deny” audit events provided to Security/Compliance for questionnaire attachments.

Example (sanitized) audit event excerpt
(Note: illustrative; actual event envelope includes timestamp, org_id, request_id, ip, user_agent, etc.)

- authz.decision=DENY
- authz.action=redwood.audit.export
- authz.scope=org:org_8f3c project:proj_19a env:prod
- authz.policy_version=rbac-bundle-2025.03.0+sha.3a91c2
- authz.subject_type=USER
- authz.subject=user_42b1
- authz.denial_reason_code=MISSING_PERMISSION
- authz.evaluator=SERVICE
- authz.latency_ms=1.7

Rollout / migration notes
- Add fields behind the same feature flags as RBAC v2 evaluation where needed, but prefer emitting decision fields even in dual-read mode (decision reflects the enforced path).
- For Private: ensure exporter upgrade is included in the recommended upgrade path; document minimum exporter version in release notes.

Open questions
1) Do we need to log “matched roles” / “matched bindings” for evidence?
   - Decision: NO for v2. Too sensitive + noisy; rely on policy_version + reproducible evaluation in internal tooling.
2) Do we log resource identifiers separately from scope?
   - Decision: keep existing resource_id/resource_type if present in envelope; authz.scope is additive and canonical.

Progress / updates
2025-02-03 (Kevin Osei)
- Drafted schema changes and aligned with Security on required fields. Added initial exporter mapping proposal. Sent to Olga for evidence review.

2025-02-10 (Allison Grant)
- Observability note: add a counter for “audit_event_missing_authz_context” tagged by service + route. This will help catch regressions.

2025-02-14 (Jordan Lee)
- Confirmed policy bundle version string format available in evaluator client; will expose to middleware so audit log can include it.

2025-02-21 (Kevin Osei)
- Implemented platform schema + emission. PR up: pr-28292-audit-log-authz-decision-fields.

2025-02-26 (Kevin Osei)
- Exporter PR up: audit-log-exporter/pr-77-include-authz-context-in-siem-export. Added snapshot tests + documentation snippet for SIEM field mapping.

2025-03-04 (Marcus Lin)
- Security review: approved. Reminder: do not add email if not already present; stable subject IDs preferred.

2025-03-06 (Connor O'Brien)
- Release note drafted for platform-2025.03 + exporter bump for Private. Marking ticket Done after deploy verification in staging.

Links to customer impact
- This unblocks enterprise questionnaire responses requiring “authorization decision logging” and supports pilots where customers want DENY evidence in SIEM (notably audit export + routing policy edits).

title: Observability-driven acceptance for streamed function-call flows and failure classification
preview: Define clear, testable acceptance criteria for structured output and function/tool-calling behavior in streaming responses, with an emphasis on observable failure classification and graceful degradation. This ticket captures requirements that the runtime, gateway, and SDKs must satisfy before we mark the feature GA. The goal is to protect downstream consumers (apps and analytics) from silently corrupted or partial tool invocations and to provide deterministic signals for operators to triage issues quickly.

title: Proxy admission fallback and snowball protection for tenant SLO compliance
preview:
Summary: Implement a proxy-level admission controller that provides graceful fallback and 'snowball protection' during limiter outages and cross-region failover. The controller sits in front of regional throttlers and enforces soft admission windows, spillback policies, and tenant smoothing to prevent cascading retry storms and preserve SLOs for high-priority customers.

Background: We've seen two classes of incidents: 1) local limiter service instability that causes a sharp drop in admission tokens for many tenants, and 2) global retry storms where failed or partially-admitted requests cascade into other regions (the "snowball" effect). Existing limiters are excellent at per-node enforcement but lack a lightweight proxy layer to coordinate temporary backpressure, spillback, and continuum-mode rollbacks while preserving fairness across tenants and regions.

Goals:
- Maintain 99.9% request success SLO for P0 tenants during regional limiter degradations by using proxy admission fallback.
- Limit cross-region spillover such that no single region experiences >15% extra load from spillback in steady-state.
- Prevent retry cascades by introducing exponential admission dampening and a tenant-level smoothing window.
- Provide a safe degraded mode that can route requests to a cheaper/quantized model variant when admission is constrained.

Scope: Proxy admission controller, fallthrough/spillback policies, snowball-protection algorithms, observability (dashboards/alerts), chaos tests, and canary rollout plan. This ticket covers the implementation and staging rollout; follow-ups will hook into dedicated capacity autoscaler and the Console UI for per-tenant tuning.

title: Audit Console: journey replay + contextual download bundles
preview: Add an 'Audit Journey' feature in the Console that lets admins and authorized auditors: run precise search queries, step through a time-ordered sequence of related audit events (replay), and produce contextual download bundles (CSV/JSON/NDJSON) that include event rows plus linked metadata snapshots (user role at time, affected resources, request headers redacted per policy). Work ties into RBAC v2 to ensure only allowed roles can replay or download sensitive bundles. Deliver both an interactive UI for on-screen investigation and an async export job API for large result sets.

title: Audit event schema v2: add stable event_id + category taxonomy (compat plan included)
preview:
## Context
SIEM-ready audit export depends on a canonical, stable audit event schema with:
- A stable, globally unique `event_id` suitable for downstream deduplication (at-least-once delivery).
- A category taxonomy to support filtering (UI + API) and SIEM field mapping.
- A compatibility plan for existing audit log consumers (internal pipelines + early enterprise exports).

This ticket covers schema changes in the core audit event emission path (Redwood control plane + services emitting audit events). Exporter-side delivery semantics are addressed in ADR(s) and exporter tickets, but we must land the schema foundation first.

## Goals
1) Add `event_id` to every audit event (required in v2) and guarantee stability.
2) Introduce `category` (and optional `sub_category`) taxonomy with clear definitions.
3) Maintain compatibility for existing consumers during the migration window.
4) Ensure PII minimization: categories must be expressive enough for filtering without needing to log sensitive payloads.

## Non-goals
- Building the audit export destination adapters (Splunk/Datadog/S3).
- Implementing the export filtering engine (that consumes `category` / `action` fields).

## Proposed schema changes (v2)
### New required fields
- `event_id` (string): globally unique, stable identifier for the logical audit event.
- `category` (string): one of the defined taxonomy values (see below).

### Existing fields (v1 -> v2 expectations)
- `timestamp` remains required (RFC3339). Also ensure we always emit `ingested_at` at the pipeline boundary (not required for event producers).
- `action` remains required (string). We will not rename existing actions in this phase.
- `actor` / `resource` remain objects conceptually, but this ticket focuses on emission and compatibility; field naming remains aligned with the canonical schema doc.

## `event_id` requirements
### Semantics
- Stable per logical event (not per delivery attempt).
- Uniqueness across tenants/workspaces.
- Safe to expose to customers and appear in logs/exports.

### Generation strategy (implemented)
- Use ULID (or UUIDv7-equivalent) generated at event creation time in the service emitting the audit event.
- Do not derive from request_id/session_id (those may be absent or reused).
- Ensure event_id is passed through any async boundaries without regeneration.

### Idempotency expectations
- Export is at-least-once; downstream dedup should use (`tenant_id`, `event_id`) as primary key.
- For internal pipelines, if an event is reprocessed, the `event_id` must remain unchanged.

## Category taxonomy (v2)
Initial taxonomy is intentionally small and oriented around admin/security workflows + common SIEM queries.

### Categories (v2)
- `auth` (login, logout, token creation/revocation)
- `iam` (role/permission changes, RBAC policy updates)
- `workspace` (workspace/tenant settings changes)
- `api_key` (API key lifecycle)
- `model_access` (model allowlists/entitlements changes)
- `inference` (admin-level events affecting inference routing/quotas; NOT per-request inference logs)
- `billing` (plan/contract/billing admin changes)
- `audit_export` (export configuration changes and exporter operational actions)
- `security` (security-sensitive configuration: SSO/SAML, SCIM, key management toggles)

### Notes
- We explicitly avoid categories that would push teams toward logging request content (prompts/completions). That remains prohibited by policy.
- `source_ip` and `user_agent` are optional and only emitted where available/appropriate per PII policy.

## Compatibility plan
We have existing internal consumers that parse the v1 audit log envelope.

### Plan
1) Emit v2 fields as additive changes where possible:
   - Add `event_id` and `category` without removing or renaming existing fields.
2) Add a version marker:
   - Emit `schema_version: 2` (string or int depending on existing conventions) at the top-level envelope.
3) Backfill strategy:
   - No retroactive backfill of historical v1 events to include `event_id` (out of scope; document limitation).
4) Downstream consumers:
   - Update internal parsing libraries to treat `event_id` as optional during the migration window, then required once 100% of producers are updated.

### Rollout
- Behind feature flag for beta tenants where applicable; however, schema emission changes should be safe to roll out broadly since they are additive.
- Coordinate with Telemetry/Observability to ensure indexes/pipelines accept new fields.

## Acceptance criteria
- [x] All audit events emitted by core services include non-empty `event_id`.
- [x] `event_id` is stable across retries/reprocessing.
- [x] All audit events include `category` from the approved taxonomy.
- [x] `schema_version` is present and set to 2 for v2-emitting producers.
- [x] Internal audit consumers updated to tolerate both v1 and v2 during migration.
- [x] PII review completed for any newly populated fields (esp. `source_ip`, `user_agent`).
- [x] Documentation update: canonical schema doc + SIEM mapping guidance reflect categories + event_id semantics.

## Implementation notes / risks
- Risk: partial rollout where some services emit `event_id` and others don’t. Mitigation: add a dashboard/check in pipeline to measure % of events missing `event_id` or `category` by producer.
- Risk: services generating `event_id` too late (e.g., at ingestion) leads to instability across retries. Mitigation: enforce generation at event creation site.
- Risk: category bikeshedding. Mitigation: keep taxonomy small for beta; allow extension via controlled process + checklist.

## References
- Confluence: Audit event schema v2 + SIEM mapping: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/audit-event-schema-v2-and-siem-mapping.json
- Confluence: PII policy for audit events: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/audit-logging/pii-redaction-and-sensitive-fields-policy-for-audit-events.json
- ADR: delivery semantics + idempotency: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-audit-export-delivery-semantics-and-idempotency.json
- Slack threads:
  - eng-security/1737021142 (IP/user agent privacy line)
  - architecture/1738703321 (at-least-once vs exactly-once tradeoffs)

## Deliverables
- Core code changes to emit `event_id`, `category`, `schema_version`.
- Migration notes for downstream consumers.
- Category list + definitions published to the canonical schema doc.

title: Console scope rebalance: prioritize per-route cost quickcards and lightweight trace sampler
preview: Context: Our original Console launch scope included a full per-route analytics dashboard, deep trace visualization, and a detailed cost-breakdown table. With limited eng bandwidth and telemetry storage cost concerns, we are rebalancing to land a compact, high-impact surface that gives product teams actionable signal while deferring heavier investments. This ticket captures the scope cuts, acceptance criteria, and rollout constraints for the trimmed deliverable: two 'quickcards' (per-route RPS/median latency and per-route token cost estimate) plus a lightweight trace sampling UI that links to full traces in observability backends.

title: Reduce join bloat by introducing shadow lookup tables and migration plan
preview:
Problem: Several control-plane read paths (tenant lookups, project->resource joins) perform wide multi-stage joins against large, frequently updated lookup tables. These joins cause high p95 read latency, lock churn during maintenance, and index bloat. Background analysis shows top offending queries hit the lookup graph via three separate foreign-key resolutions (tenant -> org -> membership) and repeatedly scan large JSON payload columns for metadata predicates.

Goal: Reduce tail latency and IOPS by introducing compact 'shadow' lookup tables that denormalize only the join keys and frequently queried predicates (tiny column set). Use a conservative migration strategy: writable shadow tables kept in-sync via asynchronous backfill + streaming replay, ability to fall back to original joins while rolling out. Deliver a measurable p95 improvement and reduce index size on the primary lookup table by ~30% through column pruning and targeted reindexing.

Proposal:
1) Create tenant_lookup_shadow and project_lookup_shadow tables with columns: id, tenant_id, org_id, active_flag, region, updated_at, shard_key_hash. Keep column types minimal (ints, small varchars, timestamps).
2) Implement synchronous writes from service layer where low risk (create/update/delete operations) so shadow is eventually consistent within <500ms. For existing noisy write paths use asynchronous CDC producer (Debezium->stream) to populate shadow initially.
3) Backfill plan: chunked backfills by tenant_id range; use rate-limiting to avoid IOPS spikes. Verify backfill by row counts + checksum queries (hash of concatenated key columns).
4) Query layer change: add optimizer hints to the control-plane query builder to prefer shadow tables for read-mostly endpoints (dashboard reads, list endpoints). Keep fallback to legacy joins controlled by feature flag per-route.
5) Indexing: create compact composite indexes on shadow tables (tenant_id, org_id, active_flag, shard_key_hash) and drop noncritical indexes on primary lookup table after verifying no plan regressions.
6) Monitoring and rollbacks: new dashboards for shadow table hit rate, staleness window, p95/p99 across affected routes. Rollback strategy includes disabling hint and replaying missing CDC events into primary shadow until it matches.

Non-goals: Full denormalization of all JSON metadata, or removal of primary tables in this work. This is a targeted, reversible optimization focused on reducing join complexity for latency-sensitive reads.

title: Long-tail quantization sweep and regression watchdog for latency-sensitive workloads
preview:
Goal: build and run a long-tail (heavy-tail) performance sweep for quantization profiles (FP16 / INT8 / INT4) focused on latency SLOs (p50/p95/p99) and throughput (tokens/sec) while simultaneously measuring generation quality regressions for representative production prompts. Deliver a regression watchdog that can detect subtle quality degradation correlated with perf wins and produce actionable rollbacks or model-variant fallbacks.

Scope: harness + dataset selection + metrics + regression detector + alerting + dashboards + playbook. This ticket covers the engineering work to extend the existing benchmarking harness to:
  * run extended tail-load scenarios (bursty arrival distributions and multi-tenant mixes)
  * capture per-request metadata (batch size, KV cache hit/miss, kernel type)
  * compute tokens/sec (aggregate & per-workload) and latency quantiles (p50/p90/p95/p99) at fine grain (by model variant / quant profile)
  * compute quality deltas vs FP16 baseline using a small eval-set (accuracy/EM, perplexity, and domain-specific heuristics)
  * implement a regression watchdog that flags quality & latency regressions and suggests rollback or fallback to safer variants

Acceptance criteria:
  1) Harness can run automated sweeps across a matrix of quant profiles (fp16, int8-q, int8-rtq, int4-q, int4-mixed) and sequence lengths (32/128/512/2048) and produce a single CSV per run containing per-request rows with these fields: timestamp, model_variant, quant_profile, tokens_generated, latency_ms, batch_size, kv_cache_used, kernel_id, host_region, error_flag.
  2) Aggregated report shows tokens/sec, p50/p90/p95/p99 latencies, instability index (stdev of p95 across shards), and quality deltas vs baseline for each profile.
  3) Regression watchdog implements two checks: (A) perf regression detection: p95 increase > 15% vs baseline with statistical significance (t-test / bootstrap) for the same workload; (B) quality regression detection: EM/accuracy drop > 2% absolute or perplexity increase > 5% on the eval-set. Both checks must be configurable via thresholds.
  4) Alerts posted to #perf-quant and create GitHub issue template with pre-filled artifacts when watchdog fires.
  5) Playbook documented: safe rollouts include canary on 5% traffic to int8, auto-fallback to fp16 for requests flagged by regression detector, and an automated snapshot of request traces for postmortem.

Test plan & workloads:
  - Microbench: synthetic micro-bench for tokens/sec with fixed seq lengths and tight loops (baseline throughput validation).
  - Realistic app mix: 60% short-chat (~32 tokens), 30% assistant chains (128 tokens), 10% longform (512+ tokens). Phase-in a bursty Poisson + Pareto long-tail arrival generator to exercise batching behavior.
  - Multi-tenant mix: run 3 tenants with different token distributions to expose batching interactions.
  - Quality eval: 2k prompt eval set drawn from production-sampled prompts (anonymized) with human-annotated EM/acceptability for a 200-item golden subset used for quick regression checks.

Instrumentation & outputs:
  - CSV per-run (S3): s3://redwood-perf/quant_sweeps/{run_id}.csv
  - Aggregation job: lambda job + SQL that produces run-level JSON summary (stored in /runs) and a rendered dashboard in Grafana.
  - Dashboard panels: tokens/sec heatmap by profile, p95 time-series, quality-delta bar chart, instability index by shard, kernel-id breakdown.

Regression watchdog design notes (implementation sketch):
  1) During a sweep, maintain baseline metrics from a designated fp16 run (same seed & workload).
  2) For each candidate profile, compute bootstrap CI for p95 and EM. Reject candidate if both (perf improvement > 5% and quality delta within allowed threshold) are not satisfied.
  3) If quality regression detected but perf improved, mark run as "tradeoff" and escalate to PM/QA with examples.
  4) Produce a compact verdict: PASS / WARN (tradeoff) / FAIL and a confidence score (0-100).

Trade-offs & open questions:
  - Dataset size vs run-time: full 2k eval gives better stats but each sweep runtime grows; need to tune sample size for daily runs vs weekly deep sweeps.
  - Statistical tests: bootstrap is robust but slower; t-test may be ok for normal-ish distributions. We'll evaluate both and document why chosen.
  - Kernel-level variability: some p95 spikes are caused by kernel scheduling jitter across hosts; attach host-level telemetry so we can post-filter noise.
  - False positives: we will tune thresholds to minimize noisy alerts; early runs will have a higher tolerance.

Rollout plan:
  - Phase 1 (this ticket): implement harness changes, CSV outputs, aggregation pipeline, and basic watchdog. Run 5 exploratory sweeps and tune thresholds.
  - Phase 2: wire alerts + GitHub issue creation + Grafana panels and PRs to console for rollout controls.
  - Phase 3: integrate with runtime canary logic so Console can auto-route fallback traffic when watchdog FAILs.

Artifacts & links:
  - Design doc (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/QPROF/Long-tail+Quant+Sweep+Design
  - Harness repo branch: https://github.com/redwood-inference/quant-harness/tree/feat/longtail-watchdog (PR pending)
  - Example raw run: s3://redwood-perf/quant_sweeps/example-longtail-20250220.csv
  - Draft Grafana dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/quant-sweeps/longtail-overview
  - Example alert template: https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra-alerts/blob/main/templates/quant_regression.md

Implementation tasks (rough):
  - [ ] extend harness runner to emit per-request metadata (Marcus)
  - [ ] add long-tail arrival generator and tenant mixer (Sasha)
  - [ ] implement aggregation + bootstrap CI module (Anita)
  - [ ] quality delta comparator and verdict logic (Marcus)
  - [ ] dashboard panels + Grafana provisioning (Ops)
  - [ ] alert GitHub issue creator + Slack integration (Infra)

Notes from early exploration runs:
  - 2025-02-18: initial sweep showed INT8-RTQ improved tokens/sec by ~38% at seq128 but introduced EM drop ~1.8% on the golden subset (warn flagged). Kernel-id "kvm-3" had higher p95 variance.
  - 2025-02-22: increased eval subsample to 500 to reduce CI width; bootstrap CI for p95 stabilized.
  - 2025-02-25: detected a deterministic regression tied to KV-cache key eviction pattern for int4 at long sequences; reproducer added to harness.

Questions for PM/Design/ML:
  - PM: acceptable quality delta thresholds for different customers (PLG vs enterprise)? Suggest defaults: 2% EM for PLG, 1% for enterprise.
  - ML: prefer perplexity-based detector or task-specific EM for multi-task workloads? We'll implement both and let ML pick via config.

Next steps: finish the bootstrap CI module, wire the GitHub issue creator, and run five gated sweeps against the current fp16 baseline. Once stable, enable watchdog for canary runs on dedicated clusters.

title: TTL enforcement for audit logs + integration points for compliance exports
preview:
Context
Audit logs are a core compliance artifact for Dedicated + Private customers. We need productized retention enforcement (TTL + purge semantics) for the audit log store, wired to the retention policy evaluation path, and compatible with evidence exports (audit-log-exporter + compliance report pack generator).

This ticket covers:
1) Enforcing retention for audit log storage (scheduled purge + verification signals).
2) Ensuring exporter/report generation can reliably produce time-windowed exports without being surprised by TTL behavior.

Non-goals
- Building a full custom report builder.
- Arbitrary per-field redaction (handled under logging standards).

Scope / deliverables
A. Data model + evaluation wiring
- Audit log storage must have an enforceable retention signal per tenant/environment.
- Source of truth for TTL: retention policy evaluation in control plane (per ADR-017).
- Cache/invalidation: respect policy cache rules; purge jobs should re-fetch policy at start of run and on retry.
- Precedence rules: per-tenant explicit policy overrides tier defaults; legal hold overrides TTL deletions.

B. Purge job implementation
- Implement an idempotent, retryable purge job for audit logs (batch deletes with safety limits).
- Job must support:
  - Tenant-scoped execution (to reduce blast radius)
  - Time-bounded deletes (e.g., delete where event_time < now - TTL)
  - Throttling/backoff to avoid DB pressure
  - Catch-up behavior after downtime (bounded by rate limits)
- Provide a manual backfill mode for one-off cleanup when enabling TTL on existing data.

C. Metrics + verification
Emit metrics required by SRE/SecEng for enforcement verification:
- ttl_lag_seconds{store="audit_logs", tenant_tier, region}
- purge_rows_deleted_total{store="audit_logs"}
- purge_failures_total{store="audit_logs", reason}
- purge_job_duration_seconds{store="audit_logs"}
- purge_backlog_estimate{store="audit_logs"} (if feasible; otherwise document how to estimate)

D. Exporter/report integration expectations
- Exporter must be able to run time-windowed exports and return partials with clear reason codes when data has aged out due to retention.
- Export should attach retention config snapshot (or reference) for the exported time window (see exporter PR-627).
- Define expected behavior when TTL < requested export window (e.g., request last 90 days but policy is 30): exporter should succeed with best-effort and include “retention_limited=true” in package metadata (exact field name can be exporter-owned, but behavior must be documented).

E. Rollout guardrails
- Default disabled for Hosted unless explicitly enabled by tier defaults (PM/Security guidance: audit logs generally retained longer than request logs).
- Staged enablement:
  1) Internal tenants only
  2) Design partners (Northbridge Bank, Photon Health) in one region
  3) All Dedicated
  4) Private (gated on installer + backing services readiness)
- Add feature flag / kill switch to stop purge jobs per region.
- Provide playbook for:
  - Purge backlog (e.g., after DB maintenance)
  - High delete pressure
  - Suspected over-deletion (break-glass: stop purge + snapshot + escalation)

Acceptance criteria
- Audit logs older than tenant’s effective TTL are deleted within SLO (target: ttl_lag_seconds p95 < 24h once steady-state).
- Purge job is idempotent, safe to re-run, and does not exceed DB pressure thresholds (define thresholds with SRE).
- Legal hold prevents deletions for affected tenants while hold is active; hold toggles are auditable.
- Exporter can generate time-windowed export packages; when window exceeds retention, output clearly indicates truncation due to TTL.
- Dashboard signals are sufficient to detect drift/misconfig (ties into ENG-18452).

Implementation notes
- Store semantics: audit logs are append-only events with event_time. TTL should use event_time (not ingestion time) to match auditor expectations.
- Deletion semantics: hard delete from primary store. If replicas/warehouse copies exist, document and add follow-up tickets (do not silently retain in secondary stores).
- Multi-region: policy evaluation may differ by region for Private installs; job must run region-local and only act on region-local stores.

Test plan
Unit
- Effective TTL calculation for audit log store given policy + tier default.
- Legal hold override: purge query returns 0 rows for held tenants.
- Idempotency: running purge twice does not error and does not delete beyond predicate.

Integration
- Spin up test tenant with TTL=1h, write events across 0–3h, run purge job, verify remaining events.
- Exporter integration (mock OK): request export window that exceeds TTL; verify “retention limited” indicator present and no 500.
- Policy change: TTL 30d -> 7d, ensure job picks up new TTL within cache TTL / invalidation window.

Load / safety
- Validate delete batching strategy under realistic volume (synthetic events) and ensure delete query uses appropriate indexes.
- Failure injection: DB timeout mid-batch; job retries without skipping data or double-counting failures.

Rollout checklist
- [ ] SRE review of job scheduling, throttling defaults, and kill switch.
- [ ] SecEng review of legal hold behavior and auditability.
- [ ] GRC sign-off on export behavior wording (auditor-facing semantics).
- [ ] Add runbook section link + oncall notes.

Links
- Initiative: PM-842 Retention controls + compliance reporting pack
- Related tickets: ENG-18420 (Retention policy service), ENG-18441 (Compliance report pack generator), ENG-18452 (Verification dashboards/alerts)
- Relevant PRs:
  - https://sources/github/redwood/pr-28489-background-purge-job-framework.json
  - https://sources/github/redwood/pr-28511-ttl-enforcement-audit-logs-store.json
  - https://sources/github/audit-log-exporter/pr-612-add-time-windowed-exports-and-filters.json
  - https://sources/github/audit-log-exporter/pr-627-support-retention-config-attachment.json

Progress updates / comments
2025-02-03 (Kevin Osei)
- Initial implementation drafted on top of purge job framework. Using event_time cutoff and tenant-scoped runs.
- Open question: do we treat “system actor” events as different class for retention? Current: no; same TTL.

2025-02-07 (Sean Gallagher - SRE)
- Please cap deletes per run and per batch; recommend default 10k rows/batch, 5 batches/run, with backoff if duration > 10m.
- Add explicit kill switch per region; ensure metrics include region label but avoid tenant_id cardinality.

2025-02-11 (Marcus Lin - SecEng)
- Legal hold requirement: hold must apply to audit logs regardless of TTL; ensure audit log of hold enable/disable is present and exporter includes a note if hold is active (even if export still works).

2025-02-18 (Olga Petrov - GRC)
- Export behavior must be customer-readable: “Data outside configured retention window is not available.” Avoid wording that implies data is retained elsewhere.

2025-02-24 (Kevin Osei)
- PR opened for audit log TTL enforcement + wiring to retention evaluator. Added metrics hooks and region kill switch.
- Next: add integration test coverage for retention-limited exports once exporter PR-612 lands in staging.

Open questions
- Confirm tier defaults for audit logs (Dedicated vs Private) with PM/Security; current assumption: longer than request logs (e.g., 180d Dedicated, configurable Private). Need final numbers from retention defaults doc.
- Confirm whether any audit log copies exist in analytics warehouse for Dedicated; if yes, create follow-up ticket for deletion parity or documented exclusion.

Risks
- Over-deletion due to clock skew / event_time parsing bugs. Mitigation: use DB server time for cutoff, validate event_time format at ingestion.
- Backlog catch-up could spike DB load. Mitigation: throttling + cap + gradual enablement.
- Customer confusion on export windows. Mitigation: exporter messaging + attach retention config snapshot.

title: Admin Snaglet: proactive prober that converts detected support signals into safe runbook tasks
preview:
Problem: On-call engineers spend significant time triaging recurring low-variety incidents that follow a predictable remediation path. Current runbooks exist as docs and occasionally as manual task templates; we lack a lightweight, auditable bridge that can detect a signal, create a safe, permissioned runbook task, and optionally execute idempotent remediation steps in a controlled manner.

Proposal: Build "Admin Snaglet", a small probe-and-task system that: (1) runs configurable probes (metrics, log patterns, synthetic checks), (2) converts positive probes into a runbook-as-task (typed inputs, allowed actions), (3) attaches a safety harness (dry-run, permission gate, TTL, approval requirement), and (4) integrates with our admin microtask runner to allow one-click or automated remediation. The goal is to reduce time-to-first-action for common incidents while preserving auditability and operator control.

title: Speculative-decoding prune: prevent double-charging when speculative tokens are pruned at finalization
preview: Background: We introduced speculative decoding (speculative sampling + early token emission) to reduce latency for cold requests. The runtime emits speculative token fragments that may later be pruned or replaced during finalization (e.g., when a higher-probability re-sampled beam replaces earlier speculative tokens). Problem: current metering sometimes charges both the speculative fragments and the final tokens when pruning/merge occurs, producing double-billing on a subset of mid-stream handoffs and pruning events. This ticket formalizes invariants, implements deterministic ledger adjustments at finalization, and instruments end-to-end tests to ensure no customer is billed for speculative tokens that were not included in the final delivered completion.

title: CI pipeline templating for stepwise pin + crypto-signed profile promotion
preview:
Problem: Model onboarding today is a mix of manual steps and bespoke CI jobs per-model. Engineers sign artifacts by hand, publish performance profiles manually, and then perform an ad-hoc pin/canary rollout. This increases lead time, causes inconsistent provenance records, and makes rollbacks error-prone.

Goal: Deliver a reusable, parameterized CI pipeline template that standardizes model build -> attest/sign -> publish-profile -> stepwise promote(pin) flow. Pipeline should produce cryptographic provenance (cosign/rekor), create version pins in the release broker only after automated canary evaluation passes, and emit audit-friendly logs. The pipeline must be usable for hosted and private onboarding flows and compatible with our model-registry artifacts and performance profile manifest format.

Scope: Implement templated YAML and helper scripts in platform repo, wire into GitHub Actions and our self-hosted runners, integrate Cosign sigstore signing and Rekor transparency log, add a 'canary-eval' step that runs perf tests against a short traffic window, and produce a single CLI command (or Makefile target) to run an end-to-end smoke run locally.

title: Permission inventory + endpoint audit across gateway/control plane (RBAC v2 coverage report)
preview:
Context
We need a canonical inventory of security-relevant actions (endpoints + async jobs) across Hosted API, Control Plane, Console backend, Dedicated admin, and Private admin surfaces. This ticket covers the engineering audit of what exists today (RBAC v1 and ad-hoc checks), mapping each action to an RBAC v2 permission string, and producing an initial coverage report (what is already enforced vs missing vs inconsistent).

Goals
- Enumerate sensitive endpoints/jobs across:
  - API Gateway routes (public + admin)
  - Control Plane services (org/user/role, keys/secrets, routing policy, model pinning/rollouts, export)
  - Console backend (admin tooling endpoints)
  - Dedicated/Private admin operations (cluster settings, upgrades, install/bootstrap, audit export)
- Map each action to an RBAC v2 permission string per ADR-014 naming/versioning.
- Identify and classify gaps:
  - Missing authz check
  - Check exists but wrong scope (org vs project/env)
  - Check exists but non-standard error semantics (403 vs 404, missing reason code)
  - Endpoint not annotated / not covered by enforcement middleware
- Produce a “coverage report” consumable by Security/Compliance and Engineering leadership.

Non-goals
- Implementing checks (tracked in ENG-18277 gateway middleware + ENG-18305 endpoint annotations + follow-ups).
- Designing the full RBAC v2 model (covered in RBAC v2 design overview / Jordan’s policy model work).

Scope and method
1) Pull route lists and service endpoints
- Gateway: dump registered routes from the gateway registry (including streaming routes).
- Control Plane: enumerate gRPC/HTTP endpoints from service definitions + router registrations.
- Console backend: enumerate internal admin endpoints used by the Console role editor, key mgmt, and audit export.
- Jobs: enumerate cron/admin jobs that mutate state (e.g., key rotation job, retention policy updates) and treat them as actions needing service identity permissions.

2) Identify “sensitive” actions (tiering)
We used the tiering rubric agreed with Security:
- Tier 0 (critical): identity/role changes, key/secrets management, audit export, break-glass, private deployment settings, routing/model rollout changes.
- Tier 1 (high): read access to sensitive logs/PII-ish metadata, configuration reads that reveal topology/capacity, token usage exports.
- Tier 2 (medium): general reads of non-sensitive config.

3) Map to RBAC v2 permissions
- Permission string format: <product>.<resource>.<verb> (v1 permissions will be suffixed with @v1 only where needed for backwards compatibility).
- Gateway routes must declare required permissions via annotations/metadata consumed by middleware.
- Service-to-service calls must declare subject identity and action; we do not want “implicit allow” based on network location.

Deliverables
- Initial endpoint/job inventory (owner, sensitivity tier, proposed permission, scope, current enforcement status).
- Coverage report summary (counts + top gaps + recommended next fixes).
- PR/issue follow-ups for the highest risk missing checks.

Artifacts
- Drive audit sheet: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/platform-engineering/permissions-inventory-endpoint-audit.json
- Canonical permission catalog (Security/Compliance): sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/permission-inventory-and-classification.json
- PRs expected to consume this mapping:
  - Permission registry: sources/github/redwood/pr-28134-add-permission-enum-and-registry.json
  - Gateway middleware: sources/github/redwood/pr-28159-gateway-authz-middleware-deny-by-default.json
  - Control plane annotations: sources/github/redwood/pr-28188-annotate-control-plane-endpoints-with-permissions.json

Coverage report (snapshot as of 2025-02-06)
Totals (sensitive only)
- Total sensitive actions inventoried (endpoints + jobs): 164
- Tier 0 (critical): 41
- Tier 1 (high): 73
- Tier 2 (medium): 50

Enforcement status
- Enforced (correct permission + correct scope + standard errors): 68
- Partially enforced (check exists but inconsistent scope/error/annotation): 54
- Missing enforcement entirely: 42

Top Tier 0 gaps (must-fix)
- Audit log export endpoints: missing explicit permission on one Console backend route used for “Export to SIEM” (scope confusion: org-level vs env-level).
- API key rotation endpoint: permission mapped but route was not annotated, so gateway middleware could not enforce (would be deny-by-default once middleware lands).
- Routing policy update endpoints (Dedicated): had legacy “admin role” check but no explicit permission mapping; will break least-privilege templates.
- Streaming chat completion route: inconsistent authz path vs non-streaming route; needs explicit permission annotation to avoid bypass/missing-check divergence.

Notes on scoping
- Org vs project vs environment: many endpoints currently assume org-wide admin; we mapped them to the narrowest viable scope:
  - Role/user management: org scope
  - Routing policy: project or environment scope (depending on policy attachment)
  - Private cluster settings/upgrades: cluster scope (under environment)
- Where scope is ambiguous (e.g., “model pinning” for multi-project orgs), we marked as “scope: TBD” and tagged Diego/Ines for design confirmation.

Proposed next actions (tracked separately)
- Convert all Tier 0 actions to “enforced + tested” before pilot enablement.
- Add CI guardrail: no new Tier 0/Tier 1 endpoint can ship without permission annotation + test coverage.
- Standardize errors: use structured denial reason codes (see follow-up PR for denial reason codes).

Acceptance criteria
- Inventory is complete for Tier 0 actions across gateway + control plane + console backend.
- Each inventoried action has:
  - owner
  - sensitivity tier
  - proposed permission string
  - scope
  - enforcement status
- Coverage report shared in eng-security + eng-platform and linked from the canonical Confluence permission inventory.
- Follow-up tickets filed for all Tier 0 missing checks and the top Tier 1 gaps.

- Drive audit sheet: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/platform-engineering/permissions-inventory-endpoint-audit.json
- Canonical permission catalog (Security/Compliance): sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/rbac-and-auth/permission-inventory-and-classification.json
- PRs expected to consume this mapping:
  - Permission registry: sources/github/redwood/pr-28134-add-permission-enum-and-registry.json
  - Gateway middleware: sources/github/redwood/pr-28159-gateway-authz-middleware-deny-by-default.json
  - Control plane annotations: sources/github/redwood/pr-28188-annotate-control-plane-endpoints-with-permissions.json

Coverage report (snapshot as of 2025-02-06)
Totals (sensitive only)
- Total sensitive actions inventoried (endpoints + jobs): 164
- Tier 0 (critical): 41
- Tier 1 (high): 73
- Tier 2 (medium): 50

Enforcement status
- Enforced (correct permission + correct scope + standard errors): 68
- Partially enforced (check exists but inconsistent scope/error/annotation): 54
- Missing enforcement entirely: 42

Top Tier 0 gaps (must-fix)
- Audit log export endpoints: missing explicit permission on one Console backend route used for “Export to SIEM” (scope confusion: org-level vs env-level).
- API key rotation endpoint: permission mapped but route was not annotated, so gateway middleware could not enforce (would be deny-by-default once middleware lands).
- Routing policy update endpoints (Dedicated): had legacy “admin role” check but no explicit permission mapping; will break least-privilege templates.
- Streaming chat completion route: inconsistent authz path vs non-streaming route; needs explicit permission annotation to avoid bypass/missing-check divergence.

Notes on scoping
- Org vs project vs environment: many endpoints currently assume org-wide admin; we mapped them to the narrowest viable scope:
  - Role/user management: org scope
  - Routing policy: project or environment scope (depending on policy attachment)
  - Private cluster settings/upgrades: cluster scope (under environment)
- Where scope is ambiguous (e.g., “model pinning” for multi-project orgs), we marked as “scope: TBD” and tagged Diego/Ines for design confirmation.

Proposed next actions (tracked separately)
- Convert all Tier 0 actions to “enforced + tested” before pilot enablement.
- Add CI guardrail: no new Tier 0/Tier 1 endpoint can ship without permission annotation + test coverage.
- Standardize errors: use structured denial reason codes (see follow-up PR for denial reason codes).

Acceptance criteria
- Inventory is complete for Tier 0 actions across gateway + control plane + console backend.
- Each inventoried action has:
  - owner
  - sensitivity tier
  - proposed permission string
  - scope
  - enforcement status
- Coverage report shared in eng-security + eng-platform and linked from the canonical Confluence permission inventory.
- Follow-up tickets filed for all Tier 0 missing checks and the top Tier 1 gaps.

title: Adaptive tenant-aware audit sampling and epoched index compaction
preview:
Goal: Reduce long-term audit storage costs while preserving forensic value and RBAC traceability by introducing tenant-aware probabilistic sampling at ingestion and an epoched index compaction strategy for coldstore.

Background: Audit ingestion has grown 3x this year; storing full indexes for all tenants has become the dominant driver of cost. Existing tiering is reactive (hot/cold cutoff by age) and causes large coldstore bills for a small subset of high-volume tenants. We need a deterministic, explainable approach that: (1) preserves high-fidelity logs for high-risk tenants and admin actions, (2) reduces indexing footprint for low-value, high-volume noise, and (3) enables efficient query performance for common forensic patterns.

Proposal overview: Two complementary mechanisms.
1) Adaptive Tenant-aware Sampling (ATS) at ingest: a decision layer that applies probabilistic downsampling to non-critical event types based on per-tenant risk signals (RBAC role changes, admin activity, SSO group mapping, recent incidents). Sampling is epoched and parameterized by entropy score: low-entropy tenants (stable roles, no incidents) get higher downsample rates; high-entropy tenants (frequent RBAC changes, recent security alerts) receive near-100% retention. Sampling decisions are deterministic per event batch using a tenant-specific seed to preserve reproducibility for audits.
2) Epoched Index Compaction (EIC): instead of a single linear index, we maintain short-lived hot indexes and periodic epoch snapshots. Each epoch compacts older epochs into shingled indices with coalesced keys and a low-fidelity summary layer (event counts, role-change deltas) to answer most aggregated queries without full scan. Cold epochs are stored in a compressed, queryable blob format with field-level index pruning to reduce tokenization and disk overhead.

Key design details:
- Risk signals: combine RBAC-v2 change frequency, SSO/SAML admin authentication anomalies, recent security alerts, and tenant billing tier. Signals are computed in the pre-ingest enrichment step.
- Sampling policy language: a small DSL stored in config to express rules like "retain 100% if role_change_rate > 5/day OR admin_login_anomaly=true" otherwise "sample 1-in-N for event_type=auth.success". Policies can be overridden per-tenant.
- Deterministic hashing: use tenant_id + epoch + event_bucket hashed with xxhash64 to pick sampled events to ensure reproducibility for audits and replay.
- Index compaction schedule: rolling daily hot indexes, weekly epoch snapshots, monthly compaction into compressed epochs. Compaction is IO-priority aware and can be throttled by cost guardrails.
- Query routing: queries for recent time ranges hit hot indexes; forensic queries spanning compacted epochs automatically trigger a transparent expansion step that either (a) runs a summary-first query hitting the summary layer, then only expands matched buckets, or (b) falls back to rehydrating compacted blobs into temporary query indexes for on-demand deep dive.

Trade-offs and risks:
- Risk of missed low-frequency but high-impact events if sampling rules are misconfigured. Mitigation: conservative defaults (retain admin & role-change events always), safety net to override sampling if a downstream alert is active.
- Increased complexity in ingestion path and compaction pipeline. Mitigation: staged rollout with feature flags and an observability dashboard for sampling rates, false-negative alarms, and query latency impact.
- Cost of rehydration on deep forensic queries. Mitigation: chargeback / rate-limit rehydration and cache rehydrated results for repeated investigator queries.

Rollout plan:
- Phase 0 (2 weeks): implement policy DSL, deterministic hashing library, and simple ATS simulator offline to validate cost/recall trade-offs using 30-day historical traffic.
- Phase 1 (4 weeks): gated runtime ingest rollout for a small set of low-risk tenants (opt-in), expose sampling telemetry and per-tenant retention view in Console.
- Phase 2 (6 weeks): enable EIC background compaction in staging, add summary layer queries, benchmark forensic query latency and cold rehydration costs.
- Phase 3 (4 weeks): production rollout with auto-fallback rules and billing guardrails, partner with support for opt-out flows.

Monitoring & SLOs:
- Metric: sampled_retention_ratio by tenant and event_type (goal: explainable 40-70% reduction for targeted tenants).
- Alert: sudden change in role_change_retention or increase in incidents missed by sampled index.
- Auditability: all sampling decisions logged at ingest with deterministic seed and policy id; logs retained full-fidelity in a short-lived write-ahead buffer for 30 days for replay.

Acceptance criteria:
- Simulator demonstrates >=45% storage reduction on identified high-volume tenants with <0.1% reduction in detectable admin/role-change events.
- End-to-end tests for deterministic replay reproduce sampled vs original streams.
- Query median latency for common forensic queries increases by <20% after compaction.

Compliance & security review: required. We must document retention guarantees, customer opt-out flows, and provide signed attestations for regulated customers that require full fidelity.

Notes: this is intended to complement existing tiering work (see dependency ENG-7423) rather than replace it.
